chemistry.reaction_name: Photochemical production of carbon monoxide from colored dissolved organic matter
chemistry.scope_summary: Direct photolysis of colored dissolved organic matter in seawater produces carbon monoxide across wavelengths 300-800 nm, with apparent quantum yields measured for Pacific Ocean samples. The reaction occurs in natural seawater under solar irradiation.

figures.caption: Figure 2. (a) CDOM absorption spectra; (b) averaged spectrum (except 40 ° N), with "error bars" shown in dashed lines. Inset: 40 ° N spectrum.

Figure 3. Log linear plots of apparent quantum yield spectra. Apparent quantum yields were calculated as molecules of CO formed per quantum absorbed by CDOM. The uncertainties of the CO production data are typically 15 to 20% at long wavelengths, and smaller at short wavelengths. (a) Apparent quantum yields from different locations indicated by squares, except for 40 ° N, which is shown as triangles | Figure 4. Log linear slopes of AQY CO versus latitude. Measurements from freshwater [Valentine and Zepp, 1993] and Atlantic water (Xie et al., manuscript in preparation, 2002) samples are also included for comparison. The negative values indicate that photoproduction is more efficient at short wavelengths; small absolute values indicate that production at longer wavelengths becomes relatively more important
Figure 7. Detailed vertical profile of k CO in a shallow, structured mixed layer (latitude 31 ° 17.99', longitude: 162 ° 39.01').
Figure 8. Profiles of microbial k CO decay constants by the incubation method at the RITS-94 station (5.5 ° S) that was modeled by Johnson and Bates [1996]. Mean = 0.33 d⁻¹.

figure_descriptions: Vertical profile of CO decay constants

notes: This paper is about environmental/atmospheric chemistry focusing on natural photochemical processes in seawater, not synthetic organic chemistry with batch-to-flow translation. Most fields are null because this is not a synthetic chemistry optimization study. The paper describes natural oceanic CO production and consumption processes.
